mnemonics.title: Cut Vertex Discovery Rule
mnemonics.content: Non-root vertex is cut if: Child's subtree cannot reach ancestor of v. Root is cut if: Has 2+ children in DFS tree. Remember: 'Non-root needs no back; Root needs many children'.

mnemonics.title: Connectivity Inequality
mnemonics.content: κ ≤ λ ≤ δ: Remember 'Kappa Lambda Delta' alphabetical order - K (vertex) comes before L (edge) before D (degree). Also: Keep Light Diets (Keep ≤ Light ≤ Delta).

acronyms.usage: Used in algorithms for finding cut vertices and biconnected components
Maximal subgraph with no cut vertices within it
Minimum vertices to disconnect graph G
Minimum edges to disconnect graph G
Smallest degree among all vertices in G

visualTips.visualization: Think of DFS tree as a tree trunk. If a branch cannot connect back to the trunk above a node, that node is a cut point. Root needs multiple main branches to be essential.
Imagine blocks as rooms connected by doors (cut vertices). You can walk between any two points in a room without needing to go through a door.
Blocks as circles, cut vertices as squares. Draw lines from each cut vertex to all blocks containing it. This forms a tree structure.
Think of graph as a city map. Vertex connectivity is how many key buildings must be destroyed to split the city into separate regions.

analogies.analogy: Like a single bridge connecting two landmasses - if destroyed, the areas become disconnected.
Like a double-crossed road network where you can reach any point via multiple routes even if one road is closed.
Network redundancy - higher connectivity means more backup paths if nodes fail.
Organizational chart: departments (blocks) connected by managers (cut vertices) who belong to multiple departments.
